industries.name: Solder & Electronics Assembly
uses: Tin-based lead-free solders (SAC alloys: Sn-Ag-Cu, typically 96.5Sn-3Ag-0.5Cu) replaced leaded solder (Sn-Pb) under EU RoHS directive (2006) — SAC305 is now the universal electronics assembly solder for surface-mount technology (SMT) in consumer electronics, automotive, and industrial PCB assembly

Wave soldering of through-hole PCB components uses tin-based alloys (Sn-Cu-Ni, Sn-Ag-Cu) at 260–270°C — tens of millions of PCBs soldered annually in consumer electronics, automotive ECUs, and industrial control boards
Selective soldering, hand soldering, and rework soldering uses tin-silver-copper cored solder wire — standard in electronics manufacturing and repair globally
Tin-bismuth (Sn-Bi) low-temperature solders for heat-sensitive components, flexible PCBs, and step-soldering processes where second reflow must not melt first joints

Ball grid array (BGA) and chip-scale package solder balls in SAC or SnAg alloys: the hundreds to thousands of solder joints connecting flip-chip ICs to PCBs in computers, smartphones, and servers all use tin-based solder

companies.name: Alpha (MacDermid Alpha Electronics Solutions)
companies.use: World's largest electronics solder manufacturer. Produces SAC305, SAC387, and specialty solder pastes, wires, and preforms for PCB assembly. Supplies to contract electronics manufacturers (Foxconn, Jabil, Flex) and OEMs globally.

companies.name: Indium Corporation
companies.use: Major solder manufacturer producing SAC, SnBi, and specialty alloy solder pastes, wires, and balls for electronics assembly. Known for high-reliability solders for automotive, aerospace, and medical electronics applications.

companies.name: Yunnan Tin Group
companies.use: World's largest tin producer. Operates tin mines and smelters in Yunnan province, China. China produces ~40% of world refined tin. Yunnan Tin supplies tin metal to solder manufacturers, tin chemical producers, and tinplate mills.

industries.name: Tinplate & Food Packaging
uses: Tinplate (electrolytic tin-coated cold-rolled steel, ~0.2–0.8 μm tin coating) for food and beverage cans, aerosol cans, and metal packaging — tin coating provides corrosion protection and food-safe surface without iron contamination of contents

Three-piece welded steel cans for soups, vegetables, pet food, and industrial products; two-piece drawn-and-ironed (DI) cans for beer and beverages — tinplate remains competitive with aluminum cans for food applications

Aerosol cans for personal care (deodorant, hairspray), household products, and industrial coatings use tinplate for the body and top/bottom domes — high-pressure rated tinplate cans handle propellant systems
Tin-free steel (TFS, chromium-coated) has replaced tinplate in some applications, but food contact, corrosion performance, and solderability requirements keep tinplate dominant for canned food
Tin cans for paint, coatings, solvents, and chemicals — rectangular and round tinplate containers for architectural and industrial coatings

companies.name: ArcelorMittal
companies.use: Major tinplate producer at its Liège, Belgium (Packaging Products) operations. Produces electrolytic tinplate and tin-free steel for European food can, aerosol can, and general-line packaging manufacturers.

companies.name: US Steel
companies.use: Major US tinplate producer at its Irvin Works tinning lines. Produces electrolytic tinplate for food cans, paint cans, and general-line packaging sold to can manufacturers and packaging companies in North America.

companies.name: Crown Holdings
companies.use: Major tinplate food and beverage can manufacturer. Produces steel food cans, aerosol cans, and specialty packaging from tinplate steel purchased from ArcelorMittal, US Steel, and other tinplate producers globally.

industries.name: Tin Chemicals & Specialty
uses: Organotin compounds as PVC heat stabilizers: dibutyltin (DBT), dioctyltin (DOT), and methyltin stabilizers prevent PVC degradation during high-temperature processing (185–210°C extrusion and calendering) by scavenging HCl released by thermal decomposition
Stannous chloride (SnCl₂) as reducing agent in chemical synthesis, electroless tin plating activation, and as food additive antioxidant (E512) in canned asparagus and fruit

Tin(II) fluoride (SnF₂) in toothpaste as an antibacterial and remineralizing agent — Crest Pro-Health uses stannous fluoride as active ingredient providing both fluoride protection and antibacterial gum protection
Monobutyltin and dibutyltin oxide as catalysts for polyurethane foam production, transesterification reactions, and silicone cure systems

Float glass manufacture: molten glass floated on a bath of liquid tin at 1050°C in the Pilkington float glass process — tin provides the flat, smooth surface against which glass solidifies. All flat glass (windows, automotive, mirrors) produced by this method

companies.name: Pilkington (NSG Group)
companies.use: Inventor of the float glass process (Sir Alastair Pilkington, 1959). Operates float glass lines consuming liquid tin in the float bath globally. Licenses the float glass process to other manufacturers — all flat glass manufacturers are Pilkington licensees.

companies.name: Baerlocher
companies.use: Major PVC heat stabilizer producer making organotin, calcium-zinc, and lead-free stabilizer systems. Supplies tin-based and tin-free PVC stabilizers to pipe, profile, film, and cable compound manufacturers globally.

companies.name: Procter & Gamble
companies.use: Major consumer of stannous fluoride (SnF₂) in its Crest Pro-Health toothpaste formulations — stannous fluoride provides combined fluoride remineralization and antibacterial action against gingivitis and plaque bacteria.

industries.name: Alloys, Bearings & Bronze
uses: Bronze (Cu-Sn alloys, 5–20% Sn): the original engineering alloy. Used for bearings, bushings, gears, marine propellers, bells, and sculpture — tin increases hardness, corrosion resistance, and castability of copper without sacrificing ductility

Babbitt bearing metal (Sn-Sb-Cu white metal): tin-based Babbitt alloy (83%Sn-11%Sb-6%Cu) lined into large bearings for turbines, electric motors, compressors, and marine propulsion shafts — self-lubricating in boundary lubrication conditions

Pewter (Sn-Sb-Cu, typically 91-95% Sn): lead-free pewter for tableware, decorative items, and pewter jewelry — tin's workability, bright appearance, and non-toxicity make it the preferred white metal for hollow and flat ware

Tin-lead (Sn-Pb) alloys for organ pipe construction: traditional alloy composition for church organ pipe flue pipes, prized for acoustic properties and workability — one of the few remaining uses of tin-lead alloy
Fusible alloys incorporating tin (Woods metal, Field's metal, Cerrobend): low-melting-point alloys for pipe bending, fire sprinkler fusible links, and precision casting of complex shapes

companies.name: Wieland Group
companies.use: Major copper and bronze alloy producer. Manufactures phosphor bronze (Cu-Sn-P) strip for connectors and springs, bearing bronze (Cu-Sn-Pb) for bushings, and specialty tin bronzes for marine and engineering applications.

companies.name: Materion (formerly Brush Engineered Materials)
companies.use: Specialty alloy company producing tin-containing copper alloys including phosphor bronze and specialty bronze for electrical connectors, springs, and precision machined parts in aerospace, defense, and electronics applications.
